title: What is Physics?
subtitle: The science that asks: why does the universe work this way?

blocks.heading: Physics: the science of nature
blocks.body: Physics comes from the Greek word physis — 'nature'. It asks the deepest questions: What are things made of? Why do they move? Where did the universe come from? Every other natural science (chemistry, biology, astronomy) rests on physics as its foundation.

blocks.heading: Physics is everywhere
blocks.body: When you type on your phone, transistors switching at nanosecond speeds use quantum mechanics. When you brake in a car, friction and Newton's laws decide whether you stop in time. When your doctor takes an MRI scan, nuclear magnetic resonance — pure physics — builds a map of your body.

blocks.heading: Observe before you calculate
blocks.body: Drop a pencil and a crumpled paper ball from the same height. They hit the ground at nearly the same time. Galileo noticed this 400 years ago — and it overturned 2,000 years of Aristotle. Good physics starts with careful observation.

blocks.heading: What if the speed of light were infinite?
blocks.body: Relativity would vanish. Electric and magnetic fields would be separate forces. Atoms might not be stable. The universe would look completely different — and perhaps life as we know it could not exist.

quiz.question: Physics is the study of:
options: Natural phenomena and the laws that govern them
quiz.explanation: Physics is the branch of science that deals with matter, energy, and the fundamental forces of nature — the laws that govern all natural phenomena.

quiz.question: Which of these is NOT a fundamental force in physics?
quiz.explanation: Friction is a contact force that arises from the electromagnetic interaction between surfaces — it is not fundamental. The four fundamental forces are gravity, electromagnetism, strong nuclear, and weak nuclear.
